IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses a relevant problem—how distributional word embeddings encode part-of-speech information—which matters for tagging, linguistic analysis and corpus quality (e.g. “Word embeddings contain rather robust information about the PoS class” and the classifier experiments). Its finding that PoS information is distributed across many embedding components and can reveal annotation inconsistencies (e.g. “detecting and fixing possible annotation errors in corpora”) offers useful practical and analytic tools. While these contributions are likely to be adopted by researchers studying embeddings, tagging and corpus annotation and open sensible follow-ups (cross-linguistic tests, hyperparameter studies), they refine understanding rather than fundamentally change the field, so impact is moderate.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's main claims (that word embeddings contain PoS information, can be used to train PoS classifiers, reveal annotation outliers, and that PoS information is distributed across many dimensions) are clearly stated and reportedly supported by experiments on the BNC (e.g. "We experiment with training classifiers..." and "at least a hundred in our case of 300-dimensional model"). However, the provided text lacks critical experimental details (no metrics, baselines, training/validation protocol, or statistical tests), contains no formal derivations, and makes some inferential leaps (e.g. from classifier success to corpus-guideline issues) without showing the supporting analyses. Given the claims are plausible and partially supported by reported outcomes but key methodological evidence is missing, the paper merits a moderate technical score.

CLARITY.justification: The paper appears well-organized (sections referenced such as Related Work, Section 3, and Conclusion) and follows a logical experimental narrative from motivation to findings. The main contributions are communicated (that embeddings encode PoS information, reveal borderline cases, and that PoS signal is distributed across dimensions), though they could be stated more explicitly up front rather than mostly in the abstract/conclusion. No formal notation is used, so consistency is not an issue, but the manuscript lacks figures/tables in the provided excerpt, making it hard to judge their effectiveness. Overall writing is clear and readable with only minor awkward phrasing and citation/style inconsistencies.

REPLICABILITY.justification: The paper states that embeddings were trained on the British National Corpus and refers to a 300-dimensional model and classifiers predicting PoS (e.g. "300-dimensional model", "trained on the British National Corpus", "training classifiers for predicting PoS tags"), but it does not provide specific hyperparameters or model configurations (training algorithm, window size, negative samples, epochs), nor details of the classifier architecture, training procedure, or corpus preprocessing. Datasets are only partially specified (BNC named but not the exact split or preprocessing), no code is mentioned, and evaluation metrics/procedures are not precisely defined in the provided text, making exact reproduction difficult.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The paper investigates how distributional word embeddings (trained on the British National Corpus) encode part-of-speech information and uses classifiers to predict PoS tags from embeddings (“We experiment with training classifiers for predicting PoS tags for words based on their embeddings”), which is squarely within core NLP research on representation learning. It addresses an audience interested in corpus linguistics, POS tagging and embedding analysis and cites relevant prior work (Mikolov et al., Tsvetkov et al., etc.), indicating proper positioning in the community. The framing and evaluation (discovering annotation inconsistencies, analysis of embedding components) are appropriate for a top NLP venue, though the contribution is more analytical/linguistic than a novel ML method, hence a slight scope concern for pure ML conferences.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper cites several relevant prior works (e.g. Tsuboi 2014, Mikolov et al. 2013b, Liu et al. 2016, Tsvetkov et al. 2015) showing awareness of related approaches, but the provided text contains no empirical comparisons against standard or recent POS-tagging baselines, nor any reported metrics or experimental conditions. There is no evidence that evaluations were run under the same data/metrics as prior work, the paper does not acknowledge when prior methods perform comparably or better, and differences to competing approaches are not analyzed in the excerpt. Overall the comparative evaluation is inadequate and key baseline comparisons appear missing.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper applies known distributional embedding techniques to PoS analysis (training classifiers on BNC embeddings to predict PoS and detect ‘outliers’/annotation inconsistencies) and cites prior work showing embeddings encode PoS information (Tsuboi 2014; Liu et al. 2016; Tsvetkov et al. 2015). Its contribution is primarily empirical—demonstrating detection of annotation errors, supporting graded PoS affiliation, and showing PoS information is spread across many vector components—rather than proposing a new method or theory. Thus it is an incremental but useful application/analysis rather than a genuinely novel methodological advance.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper shows classifiers trained on BNC embeddings (300‑dim) can predict PoS and finds outliers, but experiments are limited to this single setup and lack breadth to fully support broader claims. No ablation or sensitivity analyses are reported (the authors explicitly defer studying hyperparameter effects to future work). Related work coverage is reasonable and cites relevant prior studies (Firth, Mikolov et al., Liu et al., Tsvetkov et al., Tsuboi, Manning). Discussion of limitations is cursory (mentions graded PoS and future work) and there is insufficient methodological detail (training hyperparameters, classifier architecture, splits, evaluation metrics) to reproduce the results.
